frameworks.name: causal perturbation of cortical circuits
frameworks.one_line: uses focal inactivation to test whether LIP neurons cause decision behaviour

frameworks.name: evidence accumulation for perceptual decisions
frameworks.one_line: decisions framed as transformation of evidence samples into commitment

frameworks.name: distributed compensation across cortical areas
frameworks.one_line: unaffected areas take over function on fast and slow timescales

core_question: Do neurons in lateral intraparietal area (LIP) play a causal role in perceptual decision making?
key_assumptions: perceptual decisions involve cortical computation over working-memory / attention / planning timescales
pharmacological and chemogenetic inactivation produces sustained neural silencing in LIP
behavioural biases reflect the contribution of the inactivated circuit
manipulations: time within and across experimental sessions
key_findings: LIP inactivation produces clear biases in perceptual decisions
the behavioural bias dissipates within a session despite persistent neural inactivation
additional gradual recovery occurs across sessions
interpretations: other unaffected areas rapidly and gradually compensate for loss of LIP
compensation reconciles disparate prior causal results and constrains how focal perturbations are interpreted
